One prevailing theory in the academic world is to treat bargaining as a rational process, which is to basically pretend emotions don't exist. You often hear reference to something called Zopa, which is the zone of possible agreement, and it's the point where the buyers and the sellers' prices overlap. Knowing all this, experienced negotiators often just lead with a ridiculous offer, also known as an extreme anchor. They know this moves the zone. There's an old saying, the negotiator who offers to meet you in the middle is usually a poor judge of distance. You get punched with an extreme anchor, you lose your footing, cave in, and go to your maximum or very, very close to it. We once had a person tell us that getting hit with an extreme anchor was like watching a vision disappear in front of their eyes. This is a version of the famous Mike Tyson line, everyone has a plan until they get hit. The extreme anchor is used a lot. It's the reason for the other academic idea, which is pretty much go first and go high. This is a favorite of many of the harder-edged business school types. Why? Because the data they have is that anchoring works. It moves the zone on deals that were made. With that limited view, with those blinders on, they're right. What they don't have data on is how many deals were driven away and never made because the extreme anchor caused someone to turn on their heels and walk away. They also don't have as much data on how much money or better terms were left on the table because the extreme anchor caused people to dig in out of spite. And then ran up the cost of implementation out of the same spite. Nearly all academic studies on negotiation are based on simulated situations with students playing simulated roles. That's the only way they can obtain this mystical academic rigor. Therefore, they don't have any way to measure the losses from deals driven away, poor implementation, and the cost of damaged relationships and renegotiation. And those are the invisible losses that kill your bottom line. If relationships equal revenue, then what does punching people in the nose do to your relationship and consequently your revenue? The other thing the academics don't account for, but know is true, is the majority of real-world A players won't throw the number first. Great negotiators' goal is to gather information, actually wanting their counterpart to name a price first, because they want to see their hand. They look at the first punch as just more information. Many, many of the B players and below will go first an extreme anchor on you. Either one will probably do whatever they can to get you to start bidding against yourself. Many tough negotiators know one good passive-aggressive way in addition to a flat-out no to express no in a way that gets you to bid against yourself. Likely, what is equivalent to 50% of the third phase of no in the Black Swan system. I can't do that. And they won't let up on you until you've said no at least twice. What do you want to be? An A player or less? Either way, you've got to be prepared to take a punch and to also know how not to bid against yourself. This chapter is going to cover how to take a punch and then use the information that a punch provides to your advantage. There are several Black Swan methods you can use to weather the storm without bidding against yourself or responding with anger. Once you learn these tactics, you'll be prepared to withstand the hit and counter with confidence. The first step is to deflect the punch in a way that opens your counterpart up. Whether you use a calibrated how question to say no, like how am I supposed to do that? Or deflect their anchor by saying, what are we trying to accomplish here? Your response will force your counterpart to refocus while helping you escape the compromised trap. It gives you a moment to recover. Another way to respond to an extreme anchor is by pivoting away from the money and toward other terms. When you feel you're being dragged into a haggle situation, you can reroute the conversation to non-monetary issues that are relevant to making the deal work. You can say in an encouraging voice, I'm sorry, let's put price to the side for a moment and talk about what would make this a good deal. Or you can ask, what else would you be able to offer to make that price work for me? If the other party is adamant about you going first, allude to a high number that someone else might charge. For example, well, other firms will usually charge X amount of dollars a day until the project is completed. Know what that math is in advance as being much higher than what you're after. Then go silent. Let them speak next. Count Mississippi's to yourself if you have to. By remaining vague and suggesting a high number, you're getting out of having to go first and you're basically re-anchoring them. This will force them to head closer to their maximum and closer to your desired outcome. Even if they don't answer, you've put a number in their head. But they still refuse to give you a number first and maybe because they're only fishing and don't really want to do a deal with you. The act of throwing out a number is to make a commitment that there is a point at which the deal would be made. You can label this. It sounds like you're not really prepared to make a deal. You can also open them up by probing for a range. It seems like you have a range in mind. Count Mississippi's to yourself again. If they still don't respond, be aware of what that tells you. Lastly, you can offer a range but be aware that they will jump on whichever end of that range favors them. Make that end what you want. If they pick it, they will feel a sense of victory at having moved you there. If they feel victorious, they are much more likely to stick to the deal. Regardless of what happens, the point here is to collect information from your counterpart while avoiding being the first one to set a price. If you remember in Course 6, when you anchor a price, you're also anchoring their emotions, which is great for bending their reality. But since neither party has perfect information when entering the negotiation, if you set a price first, you might end up shorting yourself below what your counterpart expected. By letting your counterpart anchor first, you get a tremendous feel for them and use the unevenness of your information to your advantage. Who knows, you might even get lucky and have them put a number out that delights you. All you need to learn is how to handle the first punch.
